entry.title: Standard Field does not appear in BAPICCBVB BAPI_CONDITION_CONTRACT_CHANGE

I am using CDS WCB_V_MKPF_MSEG as business volume table in Condition Contract processing. We're using standard field Movement Type (BWART) as Field Combination in Business Volume Selection Criteria. 

The issue is: those standard field in BV Selection criteria (i.e BWART) does not appear in structure BAPICCBVB  in BAPI_CONDITION_CONTRACT_CHANGE. I need to use those BAPI for mass change of Condition Contract.

Any idea if i missed any configuration to make sure standard field (such as BWART) appear in BAPICCBVB ?

Thank you 

Best Regards,

Hendry

entry.title: Re: How to post negative sales in S4
entry.content: In S/4HANA, negative sales from POS should be routed to a special storage location. When stock arrives, the system transfers from the main location to this special location to clear the negative balance. This is the recommended way to handle POS negative sales without enabling negative stock in the main inventory.

entry.title: How I Connected Claude AI to My SAP ABAP System Using MCP — A Complete Windows Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP developer community has been buzzing about connecting Claude AI directly to live SAP ABAP systems using MCP (Model Context Protocol) — reading code, modifying objects, creating transports, and activating changes, all from a natural language chat interface.

I tried it myself on Windows, and it works. This blog walks you through exactly how to set it up.

What is MCP?

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard that lets AI models like Claude connect to external tools and data sources. Think of it as a plugin system for AI — instead of just answering questions from training data, Claude can actually call tools that interact with real systems.

For SAP developers, this means Claude can:

  • Read your actual ABAP code directly from your system
  • Write and modify objects
  • Run syntax checks
  • Create transport requests
  • Activate objects

All without you copy-pasting anything.

The Architecture

 
 
Claude Desktop / Claude Code CLI         ↕ MCP Protocol  ┌──────────────────────────┐  │   mcp-abap-adt           │  ← Read-only (13 tools)  │   mcp-abap-abap-adt-api  │  ← Full CRUD (30 tools)  └──────────────────────────┘         ↕ ADT REST API    Your SAP ABAP System

Both MCP servers were built by Mario Andreschak and communicate with SAP via the same ADT (ABAP Development Tools) API that Eclipse uses under the hood.

Prerequisites

Before starting, you need:

  • Windows 10 or 11 (64-bit)
  • An SAP ABAP system with /sap/bc/adt active in SICF
  • A user with SAP_ADT_DEVELOPER role (or equivalent)
  • A Claude Pro/Max/Team subscription at claude.ai
  • Internet connection

Step 1 — Install Node.js

Node.js is the runtime required by Claude Code CLI and the MCP servers.

  1. Go to https://nodejs.org and download the LTS version
  2. Install with all defaults
  3. Verify in Command Prompt:
 
 
node -v    → v24.x.xnpm -v     → 11.x.x

Step 2 — Install Git for Windows

Git provides the bash shell Claude Code requires on Windows.

  1. Go to https://git-scm.com/downloads/win and download the installer
  2. During setup, on the PATH screen select: "Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software"
  3. Complete with all other defaults

Step 3 — Install Claude Code CLI

 
 
cmd
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-codeclaude --version

Then log in:

 
 
cmd
claude

Select option 1 (Claude subscription), log in via browser, return to terminal.

Step 4 — Install mcp-abap-adt (Read-Only MCP Server)

This server gives Claude read access to your SAP system with 13 tools including GetProgram, GetClass, GetFunction, GetTable, SearchObject, and more.

 
 
cmd
mkdir C:\Toolscd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-adtcd mcp-abap-adtnpm installnpm run build

Create a .env file in the folder:

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USERNAME=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register with Claude Code:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-adt/dist/index.jsclaude mcp list

You should see: mcp-abap-adt: ✓ Connected

Step 5 — Install mcp-abap-abap-adt-api (Full CRUD Server)

This is the powerful one — 30 tools including read, write, syntax check, activate, lock/unlock, and transport management.

 
 
cmd
cd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api.gitcd mcp-abap-abap-adt-apinpm installnpm run build

Create .env (note: slightly different variable names):

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USER=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300SAP_LANGUAGE=ENNODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt-api node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api/dist/index.js

Step 6 — Setup Claude Desktop (GUI Interface)

Claude Desktop is where it really comes alive — a beautiful chat UI like the LinkedIn video.

  1. Download from https://claude.ai/download
  2. Install and log in
  3. Go to ☰ → Settings → Developer → Edit Config
  4. Replace the content with:
 
 
json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "mcp-abap-adt": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-adt\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USERNAME": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    },
    "mcp-abap-adt-api": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-abap-adt-api\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USER": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "SAP_LANGUAGE": "EN",
        "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    }
  }
}

⚠️ Windows-specific tip: You MUST use the full path C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe — using just "node" does not work in Claude Desktop on Windows. Also note double backslashes \\ in all paths.

  1. Kill Claude in Task Manager and reopen
  2. Go back to Settings → Developer — you should see both servers listed as running ✅

Step 7 — Add Free Official SAP MCP Servers

Bonus: Claude Desktop has a built-in directory of official SAP MCP servers. Click "Connect your tools to Claude" at the bottom of the chat, search for "SAP", and add:

  • SAPUI5 MCP Server — for UI5 development
  • SAP CAP MCP Server — for CAP projects
  • SAP Fiori MCP Server — for Fiori Elements apps

Also add these free hosted servers to Claude Code CLI (no installation needed):

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add --transport http abap-docs https://mcp-abap.marianzeis.de/mcpclaude mcp add --transport http sap-docs https://mcp-sap-docs.marianzeis.de/mcp

These give Claude access to the full ABAP keyword documentation across 8 SAP releases, ABAP cheat sheets, CAP docs, UI5 docs, and SAP Community posts.

Testing It

Open Claude Desktop, create a New Chat, and type:

 
 
Get the source code of program SAPMV45A

Watch Claude automatically call your SAP system, retrieve the source code, and explain it — all in seconds.

For write operations (test on $TMP objects only!):

 
 
Find a simple Z program in $TMP, add a comment at the top saying "Modified by Claude", save and activate it

For complex workflows like the LinkedIn video:

 
 
Connect to my SAP system. Copy class ZCL_SOURCE from package ZPKG_A into ZPKG_B, rename it to ZCL_TARGET, change the carrier_id in the SELECT for /DMO/CONNECTION, create a transport and activate.

Security Notes

  • Never commit .env files to Git
  • Change your SAP password if shared in screenshots
  • Use dedicated service accounts with minimum required authorizations
  • Only test write operations in $TMP before moving to real packages
  • The TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 flag disables SSL validation — use only in dev environments
  • Check your company's AI policy before sending code to external APIs

My Experience

What surprised me most is how Claude handles the entire workflow autonomously. It doesn't just write code — it searches for the object, reads the source, understands the structure, reads class includes, creates the transport, locks the object, writes the code, runs a syntax check, activates, and unlocks. All from one natural language request.

 

Important Hint

It can make ABAP-specific mistakes (like forgetting RAP locking fields, or using reserved DDIC words). Always review AI-generated code before activating in production. But as a development accelerator and code reviewer, it's genuinely powerful.

References

  • GitHub: mcp-abap-adt — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: mcp-abap-abap-adt-api — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: sap-skills — 35 Claude Code plugins for SAP
  • Blog: SAP Docs MCP Server — Marian Zeis
  • Blog: Claude Code via MCP — SAP Community
  • Blog: Installing and Extending an ABAP MCP Server — SAP Community

I hope this helps other ABAP developers get started. If you run into issues or have questions, feel free to comment below. Happy coding!

entry.title: Re: SAP MBC - OAUTH 2.0 mTLS configuration on ERP side
entry.content:
In S/4HANA 2024, transaction OA2C_CONFIG is no longer the recommended approach for setting up OAuth 2.0 clients. SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity now uses OAuth 2.0 with mTLS via SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) as the default and supported authentication method.
As a result, the configuration in newer releases will not look identical to older OA2C_CONFIG‑based documentation.

For current S/4HANA releases, the setup is based on:

  • SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) for OAuth client and certificate handling
  • Corporate Gateway / ERP Connectivity configuration on the ERP side
  • mTLS certificate management on the SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity tenant

The legacy OA2C_CONFIG screenshots in some guides are therefore only conceptually valid but technically outdated for S/4HANA 2024.

Please refer to the following official SAP Help Portal documentation, which reflects the currently supported setup:

Configuring Authentication via OAuth 2.0 with mTLS (Default Method)

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY/a0fb81f60461411ba33bc586aecd3039/23fd4011e67c4981a37a06b325991612.html

Configuring Connectivity Between an ERP System and SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY/a0fb81f60461411ba33bc586aecd3039/ab5a61b1497848dd8b6b6669340cf882.html

Certificate Requirements for SAP Multi‑Bank Connectivity (OAuth mTLS, test vs. prod)

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY/3fa51d2862684bcfa3183bf499792673/00d1af2466d849a686f345186758c21a.html

Manage Certificate Assignments (mTLS certificates on MBC tenant – App F8801

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY/3fa51d2862684bcfa3183bf499792673/2c15e2d0a1a14a9fa012e4cbbe51b8f3.html

In summary, for S/4HANA 2024 systems, OAuth 2.0 with mTLS should be configured using IAS and the new OAuth framework, and differences to older OA2C_CONFIG‑based documentation are expected and intended.

entry.title: Re: update open delivery quantity even if a delivery bloc...
entry.content: By design, SAP does not update open credit values when a delivery block is set at schedule line level. To change this, either use header‑level blocks without confirmation, or implement a custom enhancement in credit update logic.

entry.title: SAP Business One Period-End Closing executes but no Journal Entry is created (no error)
entry.content:
Hi,

I am facing a critical issue with the Period-End Closing process in SAP Business One 9.2 and would appreciate your expert advice.

Current Situation:
I am trying to perform the Period-End Closing for the fiscal year 2025.

When I click the 'Execute' button in the Period-End Closing window, the status bar shows progress (it moves), but no Journal Entries (JE) are actually created.

There are no error messages or pop-ups. The process simply finishes with no results, and the account balances remain unchanged in the list.

I have already confirmed that:

All target accounts have balances in the grid.

The Posting Period for 2025 is set to 'Closing Period'.

G/L Account Determination (Retained Earnings and Period-End Closing accounts) is correctly configured.

Background (Previous Year History):
I suspect this might be related to what happened in the previous fiscal year (2024):

In 2024, I performed the Period-End Closing via the standard tool.

However, for certain reasons, I cancelled (reversed) the automatically generated JEs and then manually created JEs to close the accounts because the tool wouldn't let me re-run it at that time.

In the SQL Profiler, I also discovered that the system executes: EXEC sp_executesql N'DELETE T0 FROM [dbo].[CCPD] T0 WHERE T0.[PerAbs] = (@P1)', N'@P1 int', 109 right before the process stops.

I am wondering if this manual intervention in 2024 has corrupted the "closing flag" or the "Opening Balance" logic for 2025, preventing the tool from executing properly this year.

Questions:

1. Does manual closing in the previous year prevent the Period-End Closing tool from working in the subsequent year?

2. Is there a way to reset the "Closing" status or flags in the database so that the tool recognizes the 2025 balances as ready for closing?

Any help or troubleshooting steps would be greatly appreciated.

Best regards,

entry.title: Deployment Issue HANA HDI: schemaParameterChangeNotSupported
entry.content:
Hi All,
We have recently performed Node upgrade from 18 to 20 for few services in our landscape.

After deployment of service, below error is encountered:
"{"error":"schemaParameterChangeNotSupported","description":"Changing the 'schema' parameter for instance '' is not supported (is '<Subaccountname-spacename-servicename>', was '<some guid>')"}"

I have observed that initially for few services the mta.yaml did not have config schema name as follows and these services are having above mentioned error:

config:
schema: ${default-container-name}
 
These 2 lines were added sometime later after intial deployment of these services.
 
We have had many deployments of these services after this addition but only now, our deployment is resulting into the specified error.

For these services, we have HANA HDI schema name as some guid and for rest others we have name of HANA HDI schema as <SubaccountName-SpaceName-ServiceName>.

Please suggest how to fix/proceed.

@anshuman_s22 

Thanks.

entry.title: Integrating Slack with SAP Cloud Integration Using SAP Open Connectors
entry.content:
Introduction

In one of my recent SAP Cloud Integration scenarios, I needed to send real-time notifications to a Slack channel whenever an integration flow processed data successfully or encountered an issue. Instead of directly handling Slack APIs and OAuth authentication inside the iFlow, I decided to use SAP Open Connectors to simplify the integration.

In this blog, I’m sharing my personal experience integrating Slack with SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration) using SAP Open Connectors, including the steps I followed, what worked for me, and a few learnings along the way.

 

Step 1: Creating the Slack App and Channel

I started by creating a Slack app from the Slack API portal:  https://api.slack.com

For my use case, I only needed to post messages to a Slack channel, so I added the Bot Token Scope – chat:write. After configuring the scope, I installed the app into my Slack workspace.

Once the app was installed, Slack generated a Bot User OAuth Token. I copied this token, as it is required later for authentication.

I also ensured that the Slack app was added to the target Slack channel where messages would be posted.

 

  • Copy the generated Bot User OAuth Token

  1. Add the app to your Slack channel

SAP Cloud Integration Configuration

Step 2: Storing the Slack Token Securely in CPI

To follow security best practices, I did not hardcode the Slack token in my integration flow.

Instead, I navigated to:

Monitor → Manage Security Material → Create → Secure Parameter

Here, I stored the Slack Bot User OAuth Token securely. This made the iFlow more secure and easier to manage across environments.

Step 3: Creating a Slack Instance in SAP Open Connectors

Next, I moved to the SAP BTP Cockpit and navigated to:

Instances → SAP Open Connectors → Create Connector

From the list of available connectors, I selected Slack.

While configuring the instance, I entered the required OAuth scope (chat:write) and proceeded with the creation. During authorization, Slack prompted me to allow access, which I approved.

After successful authorization, the Slack connector instance was created.

 

Select the Slack connector.

 

Add OAuth Scope: chat:write

  • Click Create Instance
  • Authorize Slack → Click Allow

Step 4: Testing the Slack Connector Instance

Before using the connector in CPI, I tested it directly from Open Connectors → API Docs.

I used the following POST endpoint to test message posting:

/channels/{channelId}/messages

 

Use POST endpoint: /channels/{channelId}/messages

Step 5: Designing the Integration Flow (iFlow)

After confirming that the connector worked as expected, I designed the iFlow in SAP Cloud Integration.

  • I used a Content Modifier to define properties such as the Slack channel ID and message content.
  • I configured the Open Connector Receiver Adapter, selecting the Slack connector instance created earlier.
  • The secure parameter containing the Slack token was referenced indirectly via the connector, so no sensitive data appeared in the iFlow.

After deploying the iFlow, I triggered a test message and monitored the execution using the Message Monitor.

Use Content Modifier to create properties.

Configure the Open Connector Receiver Adapter.

Monitoring and Testing

I enabled tracing and observed the message flow execution. The Slack message was delivered successfully, confirming that the integration worked end-to-end.

Using SAP Open Connectors helped reduce complexity, especially around authentication and API handling, which would otherwise require custom logic.

Try Out

 

Conclusion

From my experience, integrating Slack with SAP Cloud Integration using SAP Open Connectors is a simple and effective approach for sending notifications and alerts from integration flows.

By using Open Connectors, I avoided direct API complexity and focused more on business logic. This setup worked well for real-time communication use cases and can be extended to monitoring, alerts, or business event notifications.

SAP Blog References

1️⃣Connecting SAP Integration Suite with Slack
https://community.sap.com/.../connecting-sap-integration-suite-with-slack

2️⃣Cloud Integration with Slack
https://community.sap.com/.../cloud-integration-with-slack

Reference Links

  1. SAP Help Portal – Cloud Integration
  2. Slack API – chat.postMessage

Regards,
Shalini Jayanthilal

entry.title: Re: How to get sales order not editable after invoice cre...
entry.content: In SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud, there is no standard configuration that automatically locks sales order fields (like payment terms or Incoterms) once an invoice has been created. By design, those fields remain editable in the sales order even after billing, because SAP assumes changes may be needed for subsequent deliveries or follow‑up documents. To meet the requirement, you need either a custom enhancement (preferred) or authorization control.

entry.title: Hands One - SAP Note: 3716130 Change in Root Certification Authority for SAP Default Certificate
entry.content:
*In collaboration with: @Muhammet_Tenbih 

Dear Community,

this blog provides practical guidance on how to navigate the upcoming change in the Root Certification Authority (CA)
for the SAP default client certificate in Cloud Integration and what actions you need to take before it affects your
integration scenarios.

Intention

If you are running Cloud Integration (part of SAP Integration Suite), you may have received an email notification
about an upcoming change to the SAP default key pair certificate.
Unlike a standard annual certificate renewal, this change also involves a switch of the certificate issuer from public
CA (DigiCert) to SAP's own CA ("SAP Cloud Root CA"). This distinction is important because it requires action on your
side that a normal renewal would not.

Background

Certification authorities (CAs) are phasing out the Client Authentication (ClientAuth) Extended Key Usage (EKU) from public SSL/TLS certificates due to a security-driven industry move to separate server and client roles. (Source: Client Authentication EKU Phased Out). To ensure continued support for mutual TLS (mTLS) client authentication, SAP is proactively switching to its own CA. 

  • Non-China data centers: SAP Cloud Root CA
  • China data centers: CDC Cloud Root CA

This means the trust chain of the SAP default client certificate will change. Any receiver system that validates the
certificate's chain of trust needs to be updated - otherwise your outbound integration scenarios will break with an
HTTP 401 response.

Timeline

The rollout starts April 15, 2026, but the effective date for your tenant depends on your current certificate's expiry
date.

ScenarioCert ExpiryOutcome
1After Apr 15, 2026 (e.g., July 1, 2026)New cert signed by SAP Cloud Root CA provisioned from Apr 15. Change takes effect on expiry or upon manual activation — whichever comes first.
2Before Apr 15, 2026 (e.g., Apr 07, 2026)New cert still signed by DigiCert.  SAP Cloud Root CA only
applies on the next renewal cycle (approx. one year later).

Solution & Recommendation

Outbound: Integration Suite (Cloud Integration) to Receiver 

Option 1: Recommendation

For outbound communication from CPI to receiver systems, we recommend using the SAP-managed default key pair (sap_cloudintegrationcertificate) for client certificate authentication. This certificate is automatically renewed by SAP and — following the ongoing CA transition to SAP Cloud Root CA - it will continue to support Client Authentication EKU, making it the most maintainable and roadmap-compliant option for mTLS-based outbound scenarios.

Option 2: Custom Client Certificate (if Receiver Does Not Trust SAP Cloud Root CA)

If the receiving system cannot be configured to trust SAP Cloud Root CA or CDC Cloud Root CA, the recommended fallback is to use a custom client certificate chain. Generate a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) — either via OpenSSL or directly from the SAP Integration Suite Keystore (recommended) — and have it signed by a Certificate Authority accepted by the receiving system. The resulting key pair is then uploaded to the CPI Keystore and referenced in the corresponding receiver adapter configuration.

Inbound: Sender to Integration Suite (Cloud Integration) 

Option 1: Recommendation

For inbound communication, we recommend using a client certificate signed by SAP Cloud Root CA (non-China) or CDC Cloud Root CA (China). This is the most maintainable and roadmap-compliant option, as these CAs are not subject to public CA EKU policy changes and will continue to support Client Authentication EKU beyond May 2026.

The certificate can be provisioned in two ways directly from the Process Integration Runtime (PIR) Service Key:

  • Standard: Generate the key pair directly from the PIR instance — the certificate is automatically signed by SAP Cloud Root CA and stored in the Service Key (screenshot 1)

  • External: Provide an externally generated CSR, have it signed by SAP Cloud Root CA via the Destination Service, and store the resulting certificate in the PIR Service Key (PIR Service Key / Role mapping). (Guide: SAP Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation)

Option 2: Custom Client Certificate (External (approved/ not approved) Root CAs)

  • Approved CAs: If the sender system requires a certificate signed by a specific external CA, the certificate must be signed by a CA listed in SAP Note 2801396 (SAP Global Trust List). The corresponding key pair is maintained in the sender system's keystore and configured in the inbound authentication setup of the CPI service instance (PIR Service Key / Role mapping) (see Option 1, External).
  • Not Approved CAs: CAs outside the SAP Global Trust List are not accepted for inbound client certificate authentication under the standard domain. If a private or corporate CA outside this list is required, the SAP BTP Custom Domain Service must be used to configure a custom trust store for the CPI endpoint. (Guide: Using Self Signed certificates with SAP Custom Domain service for Integration Flow Authentication) 

Next Steps

Step by Step

  1. Identify affected iFlows
  2. Inventory your receiver systems
  3. Upload the new Root CA to receiver systems
  4. Test with the new certificate (in Dev/Test) 
  5. Activate in Production

Is There Anything Else to Consider?

  • Change management lead time is the most common risk here. Getting a Root CA uploaded to external or third-party
    systems can be a tough coordination. Start this process early — do not wait until the automatic activation deadline.
  • Professional Services are available if you need hands-on support with the impact assessment, trust store coordination, or testing. Reach out to your CSM or Account Executive as described in SAP KBA 2671448.

We'd love to hear your experiences and questions around this change. Are there specific receiver systems or scenarios
where you're unsure about the impact? Feel free to reach out via SAP Integration Services.

---

#Modernize #Integrate #Innovate – SAP Integration Modernization TEAM

entry.title: Re: How to set up the initial the Accrued Revenue and Def...
entry.content:
Hello @Feng_Chen 

Thank you for your question.

For the enterprise project scenario with scope item 6GD and revenue recognition key EPMCC, Event-Based Revenue Recognition-Projects does not provide a separate migration function that will enable your client to manually create opening accrued revenue and deferred revenue balances for a migrated project. That app shows recognised values that are associated with project-based transactions, and the balances are calculated and updated by the EBRR process, especially through Run Revenue Recognition-Projects.

Here are references for more guiding details on Revenue Recognition methods.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/7fd9ad1108b94403a2871cf348d4b891.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/23cee4ab4c5443c6b68e274e69dcf19f.html?locale=en-US

The standard supported process is:

  1. For you to migrate the enterprise project and relevant source data.

See the following references for migration support. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/444fa56a65a84453bbf5e306d754a3b3.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/20607b6de19a4d34a41b0abec6186b01.html?locale=en-US

  1. Use Event-Based Revenue Recognition-Projects app to validate the EBRR configuration on the migrated project. In the app, search for the migrated project or billing WBS, confirm that the revenue recognition key is EPMCC. Consult KBA 3656776 for more details.
  2. Use Run Revenue Recognition-Projects to execute the process for the relevant period and project section. Then review the result in Event-Based Revenue Recognition-Projects app.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/c25a797f5ccb4690a4700e324fbe8662.html?locale=en-US

Therefore, if your customer expects the app itself to create opening accrued revenue and deferred revenue without migrated project-based source transaction, the Public Cloud does not support that process. The standard design remains that EBRR derives those balances from the migrated operational project data and the revenue recognition run, not from a separate opening balance load inside the app.

Regarding your request for a blog, maybe you can have a look at the following blog which provides details on project migration.

Customer Projects Migration - Part 1: Migration of Fixed Price Projects and Time & Expense Projects

I hope this provides some details you can use for your client.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Question on Automatic Clearing Rules – Do All Selecte...
entry.content:
You are correct in your understanding. In OB74 Automatic Clearing configuration, SAP requires that all criteria defined in a single clearing rule must match simultaneously for clearing to occur. If you specify five fields, then all five must align between the debit and credit items before the system will clear them.

One option is you can create separate entries in OB74, each with its own combination of fields.

Example:

  • Rule 1: Amount + Reference
  • Rule 2: Amount + Billing Document
  • Rule 3: Amount + Assignment

SAP will then evaluate each rule independently during clearing.

On the other hand, if you need true “OR” logic within a single rule, that requires custom development (BTE/exit).

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors Latest People Profile: Workaround for Error 400 Caused by Child Object References
entry.content:
SAP SuccessFactors Latest People Profile: Workaround for Error 400 Caused by Child Object References

Introduction:

With the adoption of the Latest People Profile in SAP SuccessFactors, you may notice that configurations that previously worked in legacy screens no longer behave the same or may not work at all.

One such scenario arises when working with the Metadata Framework (MDF), specifically with composite (parent-child) objects. A common expectation is the ability to reuse child objects as value sources or references in different MDF objects.

However, as highlighted in SAP KBA 3675213 (Issue #3), this approach is not supported in the Latest People Profile:

"Referring (value source) a child object in a Parent Generic Object field is not supported."

Reference: https://me.sap.com/notes/0003675213

This blog explores this limitation in detail, explains the underlying design principles behind it, and provides a suggested workaround. This workaround may address some scenarios, depending on why you chose to reference a child object in a parent generic object that is not the same parent.

To make this clear, consider the images below:

Before enabling Latest People Profile, everything works as expected.

Legacy Employee Profile showing the "Demo Selection" block working correctly before the update.

The legacy "Demo Selection" edit dialog with a functional dropdown menu for child objects.

After enabling Latest People Profile, you may encounter an error like:

“We could not process your request as the entity cust_DemoSelection has unsupported fields. Update or remove these fields from the object definition. See details: fieldName: cust_DemoDocument, fieldDataType: GO.”

The image below illustrates this behavior, showing how a previously valid configuration now triggers this error.

Latest People Profile displaying "Error Code: 400" due to unsupported child object fields.


Configuration That Triggers the Limitation

This issue occurs with a configuration that was previously valid but is no longer supported in the Latest People Profile. The key factor is that a child object is being referenced in a field of another object, and the child is configured as “From Parent”.

SAP has confirmed in an enhancement request that this behavior will not be developed, stating explicitly:

“Any object with effective dated as FROM_PARENT must not be used outside its parent object.”

Reference: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/365909

Parent-Child Object Structure

Object Name

Object Type

Relationship / Association

Notes

cust_DemoCategory

Generic Object

Parent

Holds categories; child documents reside within this parent

cust_DemoDocument

Generic Object

Composite Child of cust_DemoCategory

Each document is linked to its parent category

Object definition showing cust_DemoDocument configured as a "From Parent" composite child.

This image shows that cust_DemoDocument is a composite child, inheriting its context from cust_DemoCategory. It cannot exist independently, which is central to why referencing it elsewhere now triggers an error.

Field Referencing the Child Object

Object Name

Field Name

Field Type / Reference

Notes

cust_DemoSelection

cust_DemoDocument

Generic Object (GO) Reference

Points to cust_DemoDocument (child of cust_DemoCategory)

 

Field details in cust_DemoSelection showing the unsupported reference to a child object.

This image highlights that cust_DemoDocument is a composite child “From Parent”, meaning it exists only within the context of cust_DemoCategory. This is a key reason why referencing it in another object now triggers an error.

Workaround: Using an Independent Object

It’s important to note that this workaround may not work for every client, as its suitability depends on why you initially referenced the child object.

Moreover, since simply changing the association of the existing “From Parent” object would result in losing historical data, the recommended approach is to create a new independent object

To maintain relationships or structure, the new independent object can be associated using Valid When, similar to how a Department is associated with a Division. This preserves the connection between objects while allowing flexible referencing with no error on the Latest People Profile.

Definition of the new cust_DemoDocumentIndep object set to "Basic" effective dating.


Add the following association in the cust_DemoDocumentIndep object definition:

Association to DemoCategory Object (Higher Level)

Name

Multiplicity

Destination Object

Type

cust_toDemoCategory

One To One

Demo Category

Valid When

 

Manage Data screen showing the new independent object linked via a "Valid When" association.

Data for the new independent object (e.g., cust_DemoDocumentIndep) can be maintained either directly in Manage Data for small volumes or using Import and Export Data for mass uploads, while keeping it linked to the Demo Category.

Updating References in DemoSelection

Once the new independent object (e.g., cust_DemoDocumentIndep) is created, it needs to be referenced in the DemoSelection object instead of the original “From Parent” child object.

Configuration update changing the "Valid Values Source" to the new independent object.

Important: Before updating the reference, export the data from the DemoSelection object. Changing the reference will clear existing field values, so you will need to re-import or update these records to restore the data after the new reference is applied.

Verification in Employee Profile

After updating the reference in the cust_DemoSelection object to point to the new independent object (cust_DemoDocument_Indep), checking the Employee Profile shows that the previous error no longer appears.

Historical data in the original object is maintained; however, the reference field in DemoSelection will be empty and needs to be updated using a mass update or re-import to restore the correct values.

Successful loading of the "Demo Selection" block in the Latest People Profile after the fix.

User profile change history showing the reference field is empty and requires a data re-import.

Final verification of the functional dropdown menu in the Latest People Profile interface.

This change must be completed before the Production Go-Live (May 15–16), as the Latest People Profile will be universally enforced with no option to disable it and does not support referencing “From Parent” child objects outside their parent context.

SAP Proactive Validation Message

SAP has introduced a system-level check to prevent this configuration error from being saved in the future. When attempting to reference a "From Parent" child object, the Configure Object Definitions tool now triggers a message alerting the consultant to the limitation before the configuration is finalized.

SAP SuccessFactors validation message in "Configure Object Definitions" warning against referencing "From Parent" child objects.

Notes & Key Considerations

  • Ensure a clear cutover date is defined, and temporarily remove employee permissions during the transition to avoid unintended changes.
  • Confirm that there are no pending workflows, as in-progress records will not appear in report/export or import processes.
  • Maintain a data backup or reference extract of the object before making any changes to safeguard against accidental deletion or incorrect updates.
  • Evaluate whether a Picklist (Parent-Child structure) could meet the requirement, as it may provide a simpler and more supported alternative in some scenarios.

entry.title: HRFORM - Sending Payslips to Individual Employees from SAP to Outlook or Mails - for all countries
entry.content:
Introduction


Are you an ABAP developer working in the HCM module, HR ABAP? Explore this blog to learn how to create a custom program for sending employee pay slips by mail.


Requirement


Even though Payslips are accessible in EC, clients will often ask us to send them by mail as attachments.

Challenges

1. There are no standard reports available for all the countries to send payslips by mail, like the one for India. 
2. Also, developing a report from scratch is very complex for HR Forms.



Steps to create a custom report



1. We are not going to create a report from Scratch; SAP already developed a report for India HINU_HRFORMS_MAIL, but for other countries, standard reports are not available.

Changes in the report: Change the default constant and parameter values (your custom HR Form name, country grouping, payroll area for your country, etc).

Code for your reference

 

CONSTANTS : gc_applogname    TYPE balobj_d VALUE 'ZHRPAYSLIP',
            gc_applogsubname TYPE balsubobj VALUE 'ZHRFORM_MAIL'.
PARAMETERS: p_molga          LIKE thrforms_01-molga VALUE CHECK DEFAULT '24',
            p_fclass         TYPE hrf_class VALUE CHECK DEFAULT 'PAYSLIP', "thrforms_01-formclass
            p_fname          LIKE thrforms_01-infonet OBLIGATORY DEFAULT 'ZHR_PAYSLIP'.

 


2. This report will work after implementing the BADI HRFORM_HRF02; otherwise, we can only display the payslip.

3. Write your own logic to send mail in the method AFTER_PDF_PRINT. Also sample implementation class is available for our reference.

Report Analysis

1. This is a SUBMIT program of the HRFORM report.

2. It needs a BADI implementation of HRFORM_HRF024.

3. Functionality is the same as HRFORM display tcode, e.g., PC00_M24_HRF, etc.

4. Designed with SLG1 log. (log gets updated in SLG1 after running the report).


Results & thoughts


With the help of the HINU_HRFORMS_MAIL report, we can develop our custom report to send a payslip as an attachment to all employees. And it would be better if SAP had given a standard report for other countries, too or else any SAP Support Note for reference, mentioning this report.

entry.title: Re: Sub contracting
entry.content:
Practical mapping options

ERP Subcontracting Process:
Use subcontracting POs for external operations, with material issue and receipt flows.

Production Orders:
For internal operations, use production orders with goods issue and receipt postings.

Batch Determination:
Configure batch determination so that the correct batch flows through each operation.

Reporting:
Use batch-wise stock reports (e.g., MB52 in SAP or equivalent) to show stock at each stage.

The key decision is whether you want one continuous batch number across all four operations (strong traceability) or different batch numbers per stage (clearer stock separation). If your client’s priority is visibility at each stage, separate material codes with batch tracking is usually cleaner.

entry.title: SAP EWM Storage Control Deep Dive: Combining Process-Oriented and Layout-Oriented Strategies
entry.content:
SAP EWM Storage Control Deep Dive: Combining Process-Oriented and Layout-Oriented Strategies

Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavor like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

1.Abstract:

This use case considers a scenario wherein both storage control processes are required at same time. In this example, two processes (Unloading + Put away) and before final put away it should through Intermediate bin.

Here Process oriented storage control is having

 #1 Unloading

#2 Put away

However, during #2, Layout oriented storage control triggers depending upon the final storage bin and move the material to Intermediate bin and the complete the put away process.

                                                                                                                           

 

2.0 Introduction:

One of the important feature of SAP EWM is Storage control process.  It is mechanism which dictate how material will move inside the warehouse with how many steps and what would be the sequence of those steps. It says stage like sequence of movement and other steps like staging, deconsolidation, value addition services and finally physical Movement among the storage types. There are two storage type control:

  1. Process oriented storage control(POSC)

This control the logical process steps like Unloading, Deconsolidation, Labelling, Quality inspection, Packing, Unpacking, staging, put away etc.

  1. Layout oriented storage control(LOSC)

This control the physical movement of material inside the warehouse, like staging area, Conveyor routing, Intermediate bins etc.

3.0 Detailed process:

As per the business requirement, we can use POSC and LOSC separately or can even make hybrid approach, which combine POSC and LOSC both.

In this use case, we are using the hybrid approach POSC + LOSC for Inbound process. POSC is having two steps Unload and Put away, however during Put away, before final put away, need to move the material at intermediate bin and finally complete the put away.

#1 Door Determination:

Configuration and master data settings are as follows:

  1. Configuration

 

 

 

 

  1. b) Master data

b1) Created below bins as staging area under storage type 9010

 

b2) Created below intermediate bin under intermediate storage type 0082

 

b3) Setting Warehouse process type along with staging area group, bay and warehouse door.

 

#2 POSC and LOSC:

  1. Configuration

 

Final storage type for raw material

 

 

Intermediate Storage type:

 

Warehouse process type:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

POSC:

 

 

 

LOSC configuration:

 

Testing:

#1) Product Master(/SCWM/MAT1):

 

 

#2 Inbound delivery(VL31N):

 

 

 

#3 Inbound delivery distributed in EWM(/SCWM/PRDI):

 

 

Step1: Below WT/WO is created(/SCWM/MON): Confirmed WT # 1712 in background, it means unloading process is completed

#1As pruduct master is having process determination as T8, warehouse process WP10 determined.

#2 Final storage type ST05 is determined, based on storage search configuration.

#3 As Product master is having door determination D001, it will determined the goods movement bin.

 

 

 

Step2: AS soon as unloading process is completed and destination storage type is ST15, so LOSC triggered and moved the stock to Intermediate bin 0821-IN-BIN

 

Step3: Finally, material will be moved from Intermediate bin 0821-IN-BIN to final bin ST15-05

 

 

 

 

 

4.Conclusion:

This scenario is applied when POSC and LOSC needs to be executed combinedly. The combined use of POSC and LOSC in SAP EWM enables organizations to accurately model both process-driven steps and physical warehouse movements within a single integrated framework.

The configuration, master data, and transactional flow outlined in this document provide a good reference for fellow SAP consultants to design, map, and implement robust storage control scenarios in SAP EWM.

entry.title: Intracompany STO with inbound ASR in Embedded EWM –Automatic IBD Creation at PGI With FU Reassign.
entry.content:
Overview 

This blog describes an intracompany stock transport order (STO) scenario using Advanced shipping and receiving (ASR) for inbound execution with embedded SAP EWM. 

A key enhancement in this scenario is the automatic creation of the inbound delivery at post goods issue (PGI) of the outbound delivery, triggered via SPED output, along with automatic reassignment of the same Freight Unit (FU) from outbound to inbound delivery. 

The solution supports end–to-end execution across SAP S/4 HANA, SAP TM, SAP EWM, and ASR, ensuring uninterrupted transportation and warehouse execution using a single Freight Unit and Freight Order. 

Business Scope and Supported Scenarios 

This functionality applies to intracompany stock transfers and supports inbound ASR execution when outbound processing is performed from either EWM-managed or non-EWM storage locations. 

Supported Combinations 

  • Outbound ASR shipping point+EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR shipping point+ EWM Storage location 
  • Outbound ASR shipping point+EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR+non-EWM Storage location 
  • Outbound ASR shipping point+ NON EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR+EWM Storage location 
  • Outbound non-ASR +NON EWM storage location -->Inbound ASR+EWM Storage location 

 This flexibility allows organizations to run Hybrid Landscapes without breaking ASR or transportation execution. 

Key Features

  • Advanced Shipping and Receiving (ASR) enabled for inbound STO processing 
  • Embedded EWM support for inbound warehouse execution 
  • Outbound execution from EWM or non-EWM storage locations 
  • Automatic inbound delivery creation at PGI using SPED output 
  • Automatic reassignment of the same Freight Unit from outbound to inbound delivery 
  • Continuous TM execution using one Freight Unit and one Freight order 
  • Updated document flow in the TM Freight Order after reassignment 

End-to-End Process Flow 

1.Transportation and Delivery Preparation

  • Stock Transport Order (STO) is created with EWM relevance. ￼

 

  • Freight Unit (FU) is created in SAPTM. 

 

           

 

  • Plan Freight Unit into FO using TM Optimizer, Automatic carrier selection is performed. 

 

          

 

         

 

  • Create Outbound Delivery (OBD) with FO.

 

          

 

  • Delivery proposal is generated and distributed to SAPEWM 

     

2.Outbound Warehouse Execution (Source Warehouse)

  • Picking and packing are executed in SAP EWM.
  •  

  •  

     

  • ASR execution at the source warehouse: 
  • Check-in 
  • Door check-in 
  • Loading of packages 
  • Loading end  
  • Delivery split is performed, if required 
  • ASR cockpit checkout and PGI (Post Goods Issue) are executed.
  •  
  •  

     

3.SPED Output and Inbound Delivery Creation (New Feature), inbound warehouse execution 

  • SPED output is triggered automatically at PGI of the outbound delivery.
  •  

           

 

  • Inbound delivery (IBD) is created automatically
  •  
  •  

     

  • Freight Unit reassignment occurs automatically:
  • The same FU used for outbound execution is reassigned to the inbound delivery 
  • No new FU created 
  •  

  •  

  • This step is the core enhancement and ensures FU continuity across outbound and inbound execution. 
  • ASR at destination warehouse 
  • Check-in 
  • Door check-in 
  • Unloading of packages 
  • Unloading end  
  • Post Goods Receipt (PGR) for inbound delivery
  •  
  •  

     

  • ASR check out
  • Put Away to destination bin in EWM
  •  

  •  

Benefits 

  • Increased flexibility for ASR processes- supports multiple combinations of EWM and non-EWM storage locations. 
  • Improved Intracompany Stock Movement- Ensures a smooth, integrated STO execution across outbound and inbound logistics. 
  • Reduced Manul Effort- Automation of inbound delivery creation and FU reassignment eliminates manual steps. 
  • Increased Data Accuracy- single FU and FO ensures consistent transportation and warehouse data 

Conclusion

  • The enhanced intracompany STO with inbound ASR in embedded EWM delivers a seamless, end-to-end logistics process by tightly integrating SAP TM, SAP EWM and ASR. 
  • The automatic creation of inbound delivery at PGI via SPED output, combined with Freight unit reassignment, ensures continuous transportation visibility, eliminates document fragmentation, and significantly reduces operational effort. 
  • For organizations operating hybrid EWM and non-EWM landscapes, this functionality provides execution flexibility, better data consistency, and a simplified logistics process.

entry.title: The Utility Clean Core Blueprint: How SAP BTP Enables Modernization Without Disrupting Regulated OPR
entry.content:
The Utility Clean Core Blueprint: How SAP BTP Enables Modernization Without Disrupting Regulated Operations

 

A White Paper by Atul Joshi

 

Executive Summary

Utilities today face a paradox: they must modernize rapidly to support digital customer expectations, DER integration, and grid intelligence — yet they operate in one of the most regulated, risk‑averse environments in the world. Traditional SAP landscapes, especially SAP IS‑U, are burdened with decades of custom code, point‑to‑point integrations, and rigid processes that make transformation slow, expensive, and operationally risky.

This white paper introduces The Utility Clean Core Blueprint, a modernization model that enables utilities to evolve their SAP landscape without destabilizing missions, critical billing, metering, and customer operations. The blueprint leverages SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) as the innovation and integration layer, allowing utilities to decouple custom logic, orchestrate processes, and adopt cloud capabilities — all while keeping the SAP core stable, compliant, and upgrade‑ready.

 

  1. The Modernization Challenge in Regulated Utilities

 

Utilities operate under unique constraints:

  • Regulatory oversight limits downtime and process changes.
  • High‑volume billing cycles require predictable system performance.
  • Legacy customizations make upgrades risky and expensive.
  • Aging IS‑U systems cannot support modern digital experiences.
  • Integration sprawl slows innovation and increases operational risk.

Most utilities want to move toward S/4HANA Utilities, but the path is blocked by:

  • 10–20 years of Z‑code
  • tightly coupled interfaces
  • custom workflows embedded in IS‑U
  • inflexible monolithic architecture

This is where Clean Core becomes not just a best practice — but a survival strategy.

2. What Clean Core Really Means for Utilities

 

Clean Core is often misunderstood as “removing custom code.” For utilities, it means something deeper:

Clean Core = A stable SAP transactional engine + an agile innovation layer on SAP BTP

This approach allows:

  • predictable billing and metering operations
  • faster innovation cycles
  • safer upgrades
  • cloud‑ready architecture
  • regulatory compliance

In other words: Keep the core clean. Move the complexity out. Modernize without breaking operations.

3. SAP BTP: The Enabler of Utility Modernization

 

SAP BTP provides the capabilities utilities need to modernize safely:

 

Key BTP Services for Utilities

Capability

BTP Service

Utility Value

Event-driven decoupling

Event Mesh

Real-time meter events, outage events, billing triggers

Integration modernization

Integration Suite

Replace PI/PO, eliminate point-to-point interfaces

Extension development

SAP CAP / Kyma

Move Z‑logic out of IS‑U

Workflow automation

BTP Workflow

Field service approvals, credit workflows

Data intelligence

HANA Cloud

Meter analytics, billing insights

Security & governance

IAS/IPS

Unified identity for customer and employee apps

BTP becomes the innovation shell around the SAP core.

 

 

4. The Utility Clean Core Blueprint

 

The Utility Clean Core Blueprint consists of five practical steps:

Step 1 — Stabilize the Core

  • Freeze custom development in IS‑U
  • Identify Z‑objects that block upgrades
  • Classify custom code into: retire, refactor, or externalize
  • Clean up unused interfaces

Step 2 — Externalize Custom Logic to BTP

 

Move the following out of IS‑U:

  • complex billing rules
  • validation logic
  • workflow approvals
  • customer-facing processes
  • meter data transformations

Use CAP, Node.js, or Java on BTP.

Step 3 — Modernize Integrations

Replace:

  • PI/PO mappings
  • RFC-based interfaces
  • batch jobs

With:

  • Event Mesh
  • Integration Suite
  • APIs

Step 4 — Introduce Event-Driven Architecture

Trigger events for:

  • meter reads
  • billing completion
  • move-in/move-out
  • credit actions
  • outage notifications

This decouples processes and reduces core load.

Step 5 — Enable Coexistence with S/4HANA

Run IS‑U + S/4HANA + BTP in hybrid mode:

  • IS‑U remains the billing engine
  • S/4 handles finance, procurement, asset management
  • BTP orchestrates innovation

This avoids a risky “big bang” migration.

5. Practical Utility Case Studies (Realistic, Practitioner-Level)

Case Study 1: Reducing Billing Run Failures with BTP Event Mesh

A North American utility struggled with billing run failures caused by custom validations embedded in IS‑U.

Problem: Every enhancement point slowed down billing and caused unpredictable dumps. Solution:

  • Move validation logic to a CAP service on BTP
  • Trigger validation via Event Mesh before billing
  • Return results via API

Outcome:

  • Billing runtime reduced by 27%
  • Zero dumps in 3 months
  • Clean Core achieved without touching core billing logic

Case Study 2: Modernizing Move-In/Move-Out Without Touching IS‑U

 

A regulated utility needed a digital customer onboarding experience.

Problem: IS‑U move-in/out processes were too rigid and heavily customized.

Solution:

  • Build a customer onboarding app on BTP
  • Orchestrate workflow using BTP Workflow
  • Trigger IS‑U move-in via API only at the final step

Outcome:

  • Customer onboarding time reduced from 3 days to 30 minutes
  • No changes required in IS‑U core
  • Regulatory compliance maintained

 

Case Study 3: Eliminating 40+ Point-to-Point Integrations

Problem: European utility had PI/PO interfaces that were expensive to maintain.

Solution:

  • Replace PI/PO with Integration Suite
  • Introduce canonical data models
  • Use Event Mesh for asynchronous processes

Outcome:

  • Integration failures reduced by 60%
  • Upgrade downtime reduced by 40%
  • IT operations cost reduced significantly

 

6. The Coexistence Architecture: IS‑U + S/4 + BTP

Utilities cannot afford a big-bang migration. The coexistence model allows:

IS‑U

→ Billing, metering, device management

S/4HANA

→ Finance, procurement, asset management

SAP BTP

→ Innovation, integration, automation, analytics

This architecture supports:

  • gradual modernization
  • regulatory stability
  • continuous innovation

7. Governance: The Missing Piece in Most Utility Programs

Clean Core is not a technical exercise — it is a governance discipline.

Key governance principles

  • No custom code in the core
  • All new extensions must be BTP-first
  • Event-driven architecture preferred over batch
  • API-led integration
  • Quarterly review of Z‑objects
  • Architecture board approval for all enhancements

This is how utilities stay modern after the transformation.

8. Conclusion: Modernize Without Disruption

Utilities cannot pause operations to modernize. They need a blueprint that:

  • protects regulated processes
  • reduces technical debt
  • accelerates innovation
  • prepares for S/4HANA
  • supports the energy transition

 

The Utility Clean Core Blueprint provides exactly that.

 

By using SAP BTP as the innovation layer, utilities can modernize safely, incrementally, and intelligently — without destabilizing the mission-critical systems that keep the lights on.

entry.title: Re: What is the difference between Actual costing and mat...
entry.content:
CK11N (Material Cost Estimate) is used for planned/standard costing before production, while CKMLCP (Actual Costing/Material Ledger) is used for actual costing at period-end to revalue materials based on real costs. They serve different purposes and coexist without conflict: CK11N sets the baseline, CKMLCP adjusts to reality.

Below are the comparison between the standard two transactions

  • Without CK11N, you lose planned cost visibility, making variance analysis impossible.
  • Without CKMLCP, Inventory remains valued at standard cost, which may not reflect reality, leading to inaccurate financials.
  • In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, some advanced costing functions may be simplified compared to on-premise, but the principle remains the same;  CK11N for planned, CKMLCP for actual

entry.title: Re: Cumulative Calculation Across Blended Models in SAP A...
entry.content:
@Preeti_G1 

You could calculate cumulative values in one of the model using Advance formula leveraging LINK syntax to lookup values from other models. Other option would be to converge the relevant data into one of the models using cross model data copy and then finally calculate cumulative using the story option or Data Action Advanced formula. Blending at story level has its own limitation as you have already highlighted in your post.

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: What’s New in Utilities: Highlights from the SAP Utilities Newsletter – April 2026
entry.content:
Dear Utilities Community,

The SAP Utilities Newsletter - April 2026 is out, bringing together the most relevant updates for the energy and utilities community, from innovation highlights and AI insights to customer success stories, product updates, and upcoming events.

 

 

This month, we spotlight the following key topics, along with several additional updates and insights included in the full edition:

SAP for Energy & Utilities Conference
Preparations for our annual conference in Toulouse (April 21–23) are well underway. This edition offers a preview of a compelling agenda featuring inspiring keynotes, expert-led sessions, hands-on workshops, valuable industry perspectives, and dedicated networking opportunities. We look forward to welcoming the global utilities community to connect, learn, and exchange ideas.

SAP Distributed Energy Resources (DER) Enables the Energy Flexibility Market
An in-depth article explores how SAP DER, built on the SAP Business Technology Platform, unifies technical and commercial data, allowing utilities to coordinate millions of energy devices, enable energy sharing, support flexible pricing, and accelerate the transition to renewable energy.

SAP Utilities Innovation Update: Insights, Demos, and Roadmap
Discover the latest innovation updates across SAP Utilities solutions, including practical demos and roadmap perspectives. This section highlights how SAP is advancing SAP S/4HANA Utilities, customer experience, and asset-intensive processes to support utilities on their transformation journeys.

 
Celebrating Innovation in Utilities at the SAP Innovation Awards
This month’s edition also shines a spotlight on the SAP Innovation Awards, recognizing utilities that are pushing boundaries and delivering impact with SAP technology. These inspiring examples demonstrate how innovation, sustainability, and digital transformation come together in real-world projects.

GASAG: Digitalizing and Streamlining Critical Processes
GASAG is transforming its core operations by modernizing the IT landscape and migrating to SAP Utilities Core using RISE with SAP. The strategic move sets new industry standards for transparency, security, and future-ready innovation. The result: cloud-enabled billing operations, stronger regulatory compliance, and a scalable foundation for AI- driven process optimization.

AI Insights from Oxford Economics
A new Oxford Economics white paper, created in collaboration with SAP, explores how Energy and Utilities companies are leveraging artificial intelligence to strengthen decision-making, boost efficiency, and improve customer engagement. As the industry navigates increasing regulatory pressure, operational complexity, and rising expectations for transparency, AI is emerging as a critical enabler of resilience and responsiveness across the value chain.

SAP Business AI in Utilities Partner Live Expert Session 
Calling all partners! Join one of our 90-minute sessions on April 13 to learn how SAP Business AI helps Utilities work smarter, faster and more productively. Gain industry insights, explore real use cases, and learn how to position and sell AI to support your customers’ transformation.

 

Get It Straight to Your Inbox

To make sure you don’t miss future edition, you have the possibility to  👉 Subscribe to SAP Newsletters

Prefer to Read It Online?

No problem. You can access the full April 2026 edition here: 👉 SAP Utilities Newsletter – April 2026 (Online Edition)

Looking forward to meeting some of you in Toulouse!

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Downpayment Payment Workflow Behavio...
entry.content:
The parked documents are not posted yet, thus there is no need to reverse them.

And we do not suggest to delete the parked documents as they already have drawn the document numbers which are in sequence. To ensure there are no missing document numbers, we do not provide the deletion functionality with the Verify Supplier Down Payment Requests

If you want to delete it, this is a new requirement to be addressed. 

SAP Continuous Influence enables you to continuously suggest improvements to SAP Cloud solutions and our most recent technologies directly to the development teams.

entry.title: SAP Integrated Business Planning: Key 2025 Highlights and What’s Next
entry.content:
If the last few years have taught us anything, it’s that supply chains don’t break gently, they snap loudly, suddenly, and usually at the least convenient moment. One unexpected weather event, a policy shift, a clogged canal, or a supplier shutdown halfway across the world can ripple through your entire network before lunch.  

SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) is built exactly for this reality. It brings together AI-driven intelligence, real-time visibility, and connected processes so you can turn uncertainty into a strategic advantage and run planning with the same precision and resilience your customers expect. 

 

Innovations That Solve Real Planning Challenges 

SAP IBP accelerated its AI journey in 2025, weaving intelligent automation deep into planning workflows so you can spend less time fixing issues and more time shaping strategy. 

Joule AI now gives planners clearer, more explainable recommendations across demand, inventory, and supply optimization. Instead of wrestling with opaque algorithms, you get grounded insights that show why the system proposes a certain action, reinforcing trust and eliminating guesswork. 

The new Excel add-in takes this a step further by bringing planning intelligence directly into your daily workspace, helping you shorten routine tasks and boost productivity without changing how you work. 

On the data side, the Harmonized Planning Area (HPA) introduced in the 2508 release provides a unified backbone for end-to-end planning, closing the gaps between silos that have historically slowed down planners. Combined with modernized Planning UIs, SAP IBP reduces complexity and elevates user experience with automation that simplifies even the most intricate planning scenarios. 

 

Proven Impact Backed by Your Peers 

Over 30,000 active users across 79 countries and 24 industries log into SAP IBP daily, running 20,000 forecast algorithms and managing 8.1 trillion planning points in 2025 alone, that's enough data to stack paper halfway around the globe.  

Business leaders across industries are recognising measurable value, reflected in the consistently earns high marks on Gartner Peer Insights, TrustRadius, and G2. 

From usability to scalability to the strength of its AI-driven planning, customers across industries describe SAP IBP as a platform that finally matches the pace and complexity of modern supply chains. These ratings don’t just validate the technology; they validate the outcomes teams achieve with it. 

This community isn’t just using SAP IBP, they’re succeeding with it. With strong peer ratings on leading review platforms and performance at massive scale, SAP IBP has become a trusted foundation for organizations determined to modernize planning and stay resilient. 

 

Where the Real Value Shows Up: Resilient, Intelligent Planning 

It’s worth grounding the conversation in something more tangible: the real‑world impact planners create every single day. And when you zoom out to see what SAP IBP customers actually move, make, and deliver across industries, the scale is staggering, and the value becomes impossible to miss. 

Here’s what that looks like in numbers: 

  • Over 30 billion gallons of drinks planned, which is enough to fill 50,000 Olympic swimming pools. From soft drinks and juices to beer, wine, and spirits, SAP IBP helps keep the global beverage supply flowing smoothly, even when demand spikes or supply disruptions loom. 
  • Over 100 million medical devices delivered annually. Insulin pumps, pacemakers, surgical robotics, diagnostic devices, SAP IBP helps ensure life‑saving technologies reach the patients and providers who depend on them, with precision and reliability 
  • Enough materials planned each year to build 10 million homes. From wood and steel to windows, roofing, and fixtures, SAP IBP supports the reliable supply of essential building materials that shape cities, towns, and communities worldwide. 

These figures aren’t abstract metrics but a reflection of how SAP IBP supports the essentials of modern life. Every drink delivered, every medical device shipped, every home built represents the work planners quietly enable behind the scenes. This is the real value: a planning platform that powers what people rely on every single day. 

 

What's Ahead in 2026: Planning Gets even Smarter 

Looking ahead, SAP IBP is entering a new era of intelligent autonomy. 

  • Agentic AI agents will help planners automate routine tasks, monitor model performance, and surface proactive insights, acting as always-on digital teammates that reduce bottlenecks and speed up decisions. 
  • Harmonized scenario management will bring orders and key figures together into a unified lifecycle, enabling faster scenario creation, comparison, and alignment across teams. 
  • For former SAP APO users, upcoming innovations (including vendor-managed inventory - VMI) and co-/by-product planning on the HPA foundation will make transitions smoother, more intuitive, and richer in capability. 
  • Meanwhile, the HANA Execution Engine (HEX) will further accelerate calculations and AI‑powered workflows, and on-stack extensibility will give you more flexibility to tailor planning without impacting core stability. 

 

To dive deeper into 2025 achievements, customer stories, and the full 2026 roadmap, download the SAP Integrated Business Planning Annual Report 2025 today.

entry.title: Mercher &Akqusition -Training bei Big Bang-Integrationen: Erfolgsfaktoren und Praxiserfahrungen
entry.content:
Die Integration von 800 Mitarbeitenden aus 42 Ländern in nur neun Monaten – diese außergewöhnliche Herausforderung stellte sich der Endress+Hauser Gruppe bei der strategischen Partnerschaft mit der SICK AG. Beim Knowledge Café des SAP Learning and Adoption Forums teilten Katja Sommerer, Leading IT Project Manager bei Endress+Hauser, und Can Özhan, Learning Service Senior Specialist bei SAP, ihre Erfahrungen aus diesem komplexen M&A-Projekt und demonstrierten praxiserprobte Lösungsansätze für scheinbar unlösbare Herausforderungen.

Die Ausgangslage: Komplexität unter extremem Zeitdruck

Das Joint Venture zwischen den beiden Familienunternehmen im Bereich Gas Measurement Development nahm im Oktober 2023 seinen Anfang. Die Projektdimensionen wuchsen kontinuierlich von ursprünglich 20 auf 42 Länder auf drei Kontinenten. Besonders komplex gestaltete sich die Übernahme in China, wo eine komplette Firma mit all ihren Strukturen übernommen werden musste. Can Özhan, der bereits seit zweieinhalb Jahren SAP-Transformationsprojekte bei Endress+Hauser begleitete, betonte die technologischen Herausforderungen: Die Migration aus einem S/4HANA-Fremdsystem in die komplexe ECC-Systemwelt von Endress+Hauser unter extremen Zeitvorgaben.

Rechtliche Rahmenbedingungen als größtes Hindernis

Die rechtlichen Beschränkungen erwiesen sich als dominantes Hindernis. Bis zum offiziellen Tag 1 der Übernahme war jeglicher direkte Kontakt zu den zukünftigen Kolleginnen und Kollegen untersagt. Dadurch fehlten dem Projektteam wesentliche Einblicke in die tatsächliche Zusammensetzung der Zielgruppe – etwa Rollen, Aufgabenprofile oder Qualifikationen.

Diese fehlende Transparenz erschwerte insbesondere die Durchführung der Learning Needs Analysis. Normalerweise bildet die LNA die Grundlage für eine zielgerichtete Trainingsarchitektur. In diesem Projekt mussten jedoch wesentliche Annahmen getroffen werden, da keinerlei Austausch mit den betroffenen Mitarbeitenden möglich war. Erst nach der Vertragsunterzeichnung konnten begrenzt Informationen bereitgestellt werden – allerdings weiterhin stark eingeschränkt und nur für einen kleinen Personenkreis.

Zentrale Parameter wie Anzahl, Qualifikationen oder Funktionsbereiche blieben daher lange unklar. „Wir wussten ungefähr, dass da 100 in Deutschland und 80 in Indien kommen würden, aber was für Leute das sind – Servicetechniker, Vertriebler oder eine Mischung –, das war völlig offen“, fasst Katja Sommerer die Ausgangslage zusammen.

Strategische Antwort: Das Key-User-Konzept als Rettungsanker

Unter diesen Bedingungen entwickelte das Projektteam eine Trainingsstrategie, die stark auf ein erweitertes Key‑User‑Modell setzte. Statt das Projektteam in alle Länder zu schicken, bauten die Verantwortlichen auf erfahrene Endress+Hauser‑Mitarbeitende, die als Multiplikatoren fungierten. Ein Key User unterstützte jeweils mehrere End User – nicht nur während der Vorbereitung, sondern durchgängig bis zum Ende der Hypercare‑Phase im April 2025. Die Nominierung und Schulung von rund 200 Key Usern zwischen November und Dezember 2024 bedeutete eine enorme Zusatzbelastung, zeigte aber große Wirkung im Projektalltag.

Learning Journeys: Strukturierte Wissensvermittlung trotz Unwägbarkeiten

Ohne klassische LNA musste die Trainingsarchitektur so robust wie flexibel sein. In intensiven Workshops mit den Fachbereichen entstanden rollenbasierte Learning Journeys, die eine klare Struktur boten – robust genug für Unklarheiten, präzise genug für den Alltag.

Die Learning Journey für Standard Sales illustriert die durchdachte Struktur: Nach der Key-User-Schulung in der Vorbereitungsphase folgte ab dem 1. Januar 2025 eine systematische Einarbeitung. Die ersten ein bis zwei Wochen konzentrierten sich auf allgemeine HR-Trainings und Welcome Days, bevor ab Mitte Januar die spezifischen System- und Prozesstrainings mit dreistündigen Prozessschulungen starteten.

SAP Enable Now als technologisches Rückgrat

Da kein Zugriff auf Produktionsdaten möglich war, setzte das Team konsequent auf SAP Enable Now. Sämtliche Trainingsprozesse wurden als realitätsnahe Simulationen aufgebaut, die Enable Now Library diente als zentrale Distributionsplattform.

Der Content-Erstellungszeitraum war extrem begrenzt: Zwischen Mitte September und Ende Oktober mussten alle Trainingsmaterialien fertiggestellt werden. Um die Prozessexperten zu entlasten, wurden Content Creators über SAP engagiert – eine Entscheidung, die zwar teuer war, aber unter dem Zeitdruck "hervorragend funktioniert" hat, so Katja Sommerer.

Erfolgsfaktoren und organisatorische Unterstützung

Der Projekterfolg basierte auf mehreren kritischen Faktoren. "Wir hatten Top Management Attention auf Tagesbasis. Alle involvierten Mitarbeiter weltweit handelten konzentriert darauf, diese 860 Menschen an Bord zu nehmen", erläuterte Katja Sommerer die organisatorische Unterstützung.

Die Nutzung bestehender Community-Strukturen erwies sich als besonders klug. Fulfillment-, Logistik- und Finance-Communities wurden um neue Mitarbeitende erweitert und durch Projektteam-Mitglieder verstärkt. Der Austausch erfolgte über Microsoft Teams-Channels, wobei regionale Vernetzung besonders erfolgreich war.

Ein durchdachtes Ticketing-System sorgte für klare Eskalationswege: "Wenn ihr das Gefühl habt, das ist ein IT-Thema, dann macht ein Ticket auf. Wenn ihr bloß nicht wisst, wie es geht, dann nutzt die Community." Diese Logik bewährte sich trotz anfänglicher Skepsis überraschend gut.

Herausforderungen bei der Rollenzuordnung

Die Zuordnung neuer Mitarbeitender zu vordefinierten Trainingsrollen gestaltete sich komplex. "Die Jobtitel hießen völlig anders als unsere Rollen", beschrieb Katja Sommerer die Situation. Die Lösung lag in einer pragmatischen Herangehensweise mit flexibler Rollenzuordnung basierend auf Endress+Hauser-Standards, unterstützt durch öffentlich zugängliche Trainingsinhalte.

Messbare Erfolgsindikatoren

Zum Go-Live-Termin am 1. März 2025 waren alle 860 neuen Mitarbeitenden vollständig integriert: "Jeder hatte seinen Laptop, die passenden Berechtigungen, seine Zugriffsdaten, die Stammdaten waren sauber gepflegt, das Business war übernommen, sie konnten arbeiten." Die Datenintegration gelang trotz komplexer Systemlandschaft ohne größere Störungen, die Hypercare-Phase bis April 2025 verlief erfolgreich.

Skalierbarkeit und Übertragbarkeit

Die Methodik demonstriert, wie komplexeste M&A-Integrationen erfolgreich bewältigt werden können. Die Kombination aus Key-User-Konzept, rollenbasierten Learning Journeys und modernen Trainingstechnologien schafft ein reproduzierbares Framework. Besonders bemerkenswert ist die Flexibilität des Ansatzes, der trotz unvollständiger Informationen funktionierte.

Fazit

Die Erfahrungen von Katja Sommerer und Can Özhan demonstrieren, dass erfolgreiche M&A-Trainings weniger eine Frage der verfügbaren Zeit als vielmehr der strategischen Planung und konsequenten Umsetzung sind. Das Key-User-Konzept erweist sich als wertvoller Baustein für nachhaltige Organisationsentwicklung.

Für Unternehmen vor ähnlichen M&A-Herausforderungen bietet dieses Praxisbeispiel eine gute Blaupause. Die Kombination aus vorausschauender Planung, flexiblen Trainingskonzepten und nachhaltiger Betreuung schafft die Grundlage für erfolgreiche Integrationen – auch unter extremem Zeitdruck und regulatorischen Beschränkungen.

Hier geht es zu den Slides zu der Session. Das Forum 2026 steht auch schon in den Startlöchern - hier geht es zur Agenda und zur Registrierung.

entry.title: Context-Driven Dashboard Experiences: Role-Based Layouts in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
What if a single dashboard could completely change depending on who’s viewing it?

While working on a recent project, I explored how scripting can be used to tailor dashboards dynamically based on user roles. Instead of maintaining multiple versions of the same story, I built a single dashboard that adapts in real time – showing only the widgets relevant to each user.

The approach is straightforward: start with a story where all widgets are hidden by default, then use a script to detect the user’s role on load and reveal the appropriate content. This can be as simple as adding a few extra charts, or as powerful as presenting an entirely different dashboard experience.

In this example, I focused on contextualisation by designing views for two distinct audiences – Sales and Marketing – each with their own KPIs.

The API Reference Guide is a great place to start and understand the scripting possibilities: View API Reference

This blog is the first in a series of exploring applied use cases in SAP Analytics Cloud. 

1. Create the Dashboard

I began by building the Sales view, which includes charts, a contextualised dashboard summary, and dynamic name and team text. Once this was in place, I hid all the containers within the page outline except for the dynamic name and team text.

Next, I created the Marketing view, following the same approach – building out the required components and then hiding all the relevant containers. At this stage, when the story is opened, the dashboard appears blank regardless of the user’s role.

Note: I made sure to label the containers (Sales1, Sales2, etc.) so it was easier to find what I wanted to show. 

 

2. Display Content Based on User Role

With the layout complete, I moved on to scripting.

 

Using the on-initialisation script, I first created a variable to capture the user’s team information. I then defined another variable to extract the specific team name. In addition, I created a global variable called “Team” within the page outline, which allows the team name to be displayed dynamically at the top of the dashboard.

 

var teams = Application.getTeamsInfo();
var teamName = teams[0].name;
Team = teams[0].name;

From there, it was simply a matter of using an if statement to control visibility – setting the appropriate elements to visible based on the user’s team. As a result, when a user logs in, they only see the content relevant to them, with the team name updating dynamically. 

if (teamName === "Sales") {
    Sales.setVisible(true);
    Sales1.setVisible(true);
    Sales2.setVisible(true);
    Sales3.setVisible(true);
    Sales4.setVisible(true);
}

if (teamName === "Marketing") {
    Marketing.setVisible(true);
    Marketing1.setVisible(true);
    Marketing2.setVisible(true);
    Marketing3.setVisible(true);
    Marketing4.setVisible(true);
}

 

There are plenty of opportunities for further customisation here. Beyond just showing or hiding charts, you can tailor buttons, apply filters, and even switch branding to create a more personalised experience.

entry.title: F17P cannot consolidate balance reports across multiple company codes (Ford Blue, Pro, and Model-e).
entry.content:

When the Balance Confirmation process is executed using T-code F17P specifically for Poland and CZECH, the system generates individual reports restricted to a single company code. This results in fragmented reporting for a single customer entity. There is a business requirement to provide these customers with a single, unified balance confirmation statement that aggregates their financial position across all relevant Ford company codes, rather than receiving three separate documents.
